Haydn's Mass in Time of War is alternately known as the "Paukenmesse" (POW-ken-mess-uh) due to its ominous solo for this instrument in the Agnus Dei. Two sets of these instruments play dueling glissandi in the final movement of Carl Nielsen's Inextinguishable Symphony.

This 1798 mass is scored for no woodwinds, owing to Nikolaus II Esterházy having recently dismissed his wind band. It gained both its official title and its common nickname from contemporary events in the Napoleonic wars.

"The Lark" is the title of a Joseph Haydn piece in this genre, part of a collection named for the violinist Johann Tost. Other collections of these works by Haydn include the Opus 20 "Sun" ones, the Opus 33 "Russian" ones, and the Opus 76 "Erdődy" (AIR-der-dee) ones.

A song called "The Lark", later transcribed for piano by Mily Balakirev (buh-LAH-kee-riff), is the tenth piece in this composer's A Farewell to St. Petersburg. In one of his operas, the title couple is reunited after the exposure of the kidnapping scheme carried out by Farlaf.
